summarization.a: [Top panel] Grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction pattern of the iron oxide thin film grown at 500 °C, showing diffraction peaks characteristic of predominantly hematite (α-Fe₂O₃).
summarization.b: [Middle panel] Grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction pattern of the iron oxide thin film grown at 400 °C, indicating hematite as the main phase in the as-deposited film with no clear contribution from other iron oxide phases.
summarization.c: [Bottom panel] Grazing-incidence X-ray diffraction pattern of the iron oxide thin film grown at 375 °C, exhibiting reflections mainly attributed to magnetite (Fe₃O₄) with possible minor contributions from maghemite.

d.question: Why is controlling the iron oxide phase important for thin-film applications?
d.answer: Different iron oxide phases exhibit distinct magnetic, electrical, and chemical properties. Hematite is more thermodynamically stable and suitable for high-temperature or photocatalytic applications, while magnetite offers higher electrical conductivity and magnetic responsiveness, which are valuable for sensing, spintronic, and catalytic applications. The ability to tune the phase through deposition temperature enables targeted optimization of film performance for specific technological uses.

c.question: What structural phases of iron oxide are identified in the films grown at different temperatures?
c.answer: Hematite (α-Fe₂O₃) at 500 °C, Hematite (α-Fe₂O₃) at 400 °C, Magnetite (Fe₃O₄), with possible maghemite contribution, at 375 °C
